question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the chained reactive operators in listen() (.filter().doOnNext()) where no operator enforces message ordering, allowing the underlying listener to emit messages in any order and have them all pass through to the subscriber, resulting in a reorganized transaction history being presented as authoritative?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() compares consensusTimestamp as a raw long integer without timezone or epoch normalization, allowing a crafted filter with a startTime that exploits epoch boundary conditions to include or exclude specific messages, reorganizing the visible history around those boundaries?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a .sort() or .ordered() operator in the listen() Flux pipeline to receive messages from the REDIS listener type in the order they were published to Redis channels rather than consensus order, causing the subscriber to see a reorganized transaction history that reflects Redis publication order instead of ledger order?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not check for message.getSequenceNumber() > previousSequenceNumber, allowing a scenario where a message with sequence number 5 is delivered after sequence number 10, and both pass the filter, presenting a reorganized sequence to the subscriber?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Flux returned by listen() has no backpressure-enforced ordering guarantee, meaning that under high load, messages may be buffered and reordered by the reactive scheduler before reaching the subscriber, causing the subscriber to see a reorganized transaction history without any indication that reordering occurred?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() uses message.getTopicId().equals() (object equality via EntityId) without verifying that the topicId has not been aliased or remapped, allowing messages from a different but aliased topic to pass the filter and be interleaved with the target topic's history, reorganizing the transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not validate the integrity of the TopicMessageFilter before passing it to getTopicListener().listen(filter), allowing a filter with a manipulated subscriberId to be used to correlate and merge message streams from multiple subscriptions, creating a composite view that reorganizes transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that recordMetric() uses System.currentTimeMillis() (wall clock) rather than a monotonic clock, allowing an attacker who can influence system time (e.g., via NTP manipulation in a shared environment) to cause latency metrics to be recorded as negative or zero, suppressing metric recording and hiding evidence of message reordering?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not enforce that messages are delivered in strictly ascending consensusTimestamp order, allowing a scenario where two messages with the same consensusTimestamp (possible in some edge cases) are both delivered, and the subscriber cannot determine their true ordering, effectively reorganizing the history at that timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not apply any .distinct() or deduplication operator to the Flux, allowing the same TopicMessage to be emitted multiple times by the underlying listener (e.g., due to Redis retry logic) and delivered to the subscriber, making a single transaction appear multiple times in the history stream?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate the message's chunkInfo field, allowing chunked messages to be delivered out of chunk order, causing the subscriber to reconstruct a message from chunks in the wrong sequence and misinterpret the transaction content, effectively reorganizing the data within a single transaction?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() delegates to getTopicListener() which returns a stateful listener (e.g., SharedPollingTopicListener with shared PollingContext), and by opening a subscription at a specific time, influence the shared lastConsensusTimestamp state in a way that causes other subscribers to miss messages, creating gaps in their transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not check whether the message's consensusTimestamp is within a reasonable range of the current time, allowing very old historical messages (with consensusTimestamp far in the past) to be injected into a live stream and appear as if they are current transactions, reorganizing the perceived timeline?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not apply a .timeout() operator to the Flux, allowing a slow subscriber to hold a subscription open indefinitely and accumulate messages that, when finally consumed, appear to represent a reorganized history due to the time elapsed since they were emitted?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate the payerAccountId or memo fields of TopicMessage, allowing messages with manipulated metadata to pass through the filter and be presented to subscribers as authentic transactions, enabling the attacker to reorganize the apparent authorship of transactions in the history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not enforce a minimum consensusTimestamp gap between consecutive messages, allowing messages with identical or near-identical timestamps to be delivered in an arbitrary order determined by the underlying listener, reorganizing the transaction history at fine-grained time boundaries?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not cross-validate the message's topicId against the message's sequenceNumber namespace, allowing a message from a different topic that happens to have the same topicId encoding to pass the filter and be inserted into the target topic's history stream, reorganizing the sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not apply a .sort(Comparator.comparingLong(TopicMessage::getConsensusTimestamp)) operator, meaning that if the underlying PollingTopicListener or RedisTopicListener emits messages out of consensus order due to database query behavior or Redis channel ordering, the subscriber receives a reorganized history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() uses a simple >= comparison for consensusTimestamp without considering nanosecond precision edge cases, where two messages with consensusTimestamps differing by less than 1 nanosecond could be ordered differently by different listener implementations, causing inconsistent history views across subscriptions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not validate that the TopicMessageFilter's endTime is consistent with the messages being delivered, allowing messages with consensusTimestamp beyond the specified endTime to be delivered if the underlying listener does not enforce the endTime, reorganizing the history by including out-of-window transactions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate the message's runningHash against the previous message's runningHash, allowing a stream of messages with a broken hash chain to be delivered to the subscriber, who cannot detect that the transaction history has been tampered with or reorganized?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not apply rate limiting per topicId, allowing the attacker to flood the system with subscription requests for the same topic, causing the underlying listener to be overwhelmed and emit messages in a degraded, potentially reordered fashion that reorganizes the transaction history for all subscribers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not enforce that the message's sequenceNumber is exactly one greater than the previously delivered message's sequenceNumber, allowing the subscriber to receive a stream with sequence number gaps (e.g., 1, 2, 5, 6) without any error signal, making it impossible to detect that transactions 3 and 4 have been omitted from the history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not apply a .windowUntilChanged() or similar operator to detect when the consensusTimestamp decreases, allowing a message with a lower consensusTimestamp than the previous message to be silently delivered, causing the subscriber to see a history where time appears to go backwards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate the message's topicSequenceNumber against a server-side counter, allowing a message with a manipulated sequence number to pass the filter and be delivered to the subscriber, who may use the sequence number to reconstruct history and thus receive a reorganized view?
